input: I run a software development agency and need to draft a Master Service Agreement for client engagements. What sections should it include and what are the critical terms?
ideal: Response should cover the full MSA structure: definitions, scope of services referencing SOWs, payment terms (Net-30, late fees at 1.5%/month), IP ownership (client owns deliverables upon full payment, provider retains pre-existing IP with license-back), confidentiality (mutual, 5 standard exclusions), representations and warranties, mutual indemnification, limitation of liability (12-month fee cap, consequential damages exclusion), term and termination (for cause with 30-day cure, for convenience with notice), dispute resolution (escalation path: negotiation > mediation > arbitration), and general provisions (force majeure, assignment, severability, survival). Should emphasize the MSA > SOW > PO hierarchy and explain that legal terms are negotiated once in the MSA so individual project SOWs only contain scope, timeline, and fees. Must include the disclaimer that templates require attorney review before use.

input: We're about to discuss a potential partnership with another company and need an NDA. Should it be mutual or unilateral? What key terms should we include?
ideal: Response should recommend a mutual NDA for partnership discussions since both parties will share confidential information. Should cover: broad definition of confidential information (business, technical, financial, customer data plus catch-all), the five standard exclusions (publicly available, prior knowledge, independent development, third-party receipt, legal compulsion with notice), obligations (protect with same care as own, need-to-know access only), term (2-3 years for the NDA, confidentiality survival 2-5 years, trade secrets indefinite), return/destroy within 30 days with carve-out for automated backups, remedies including injunctive relief acknowledgment, and general provisions. Should note that mutual NDAs signal good faith in partnership discussions.

input: I'm a managed services provider and need to design SLA tiers for my clients. What should I include and how do I structure the credit system?
ideal: Response should provide tiered SLA structure: Standard (99.5% uptime, P1 30min response, P2 2hr response), Professional (99.9%, P1 15min, P2 1hr), Enterprise (99.95%, P1 15min, P2 30min, dedicated account manager). Should cover measurement methodology (1-minute intervals from provider infrastructure boundary, exclude scheduled maintenance with 72hr advance notice), severity definitions (P1-P4 with examples), response vs resolution time distinction, credit structure (5-10% per 0.1% miss, capped at 30% monthly fees), automatic credit application vs claims-based (recommend automatic), exclusions (scheduled maintenance, force majeure, client-caused issues), and reporting obligations (monthly uptime reports, dashboard access). Should warn against 'The Paper Shield' anti-pattern of SLAs with no meaningful remedies.

input: I'm hiring a freelance designer for a brand identity project. What should the contractor agreement include, especially around IP?
ideal: Response should cover the full freelancer agreement structure: engagement description with explicit independent contractor declaration, scope and deliverables with measurable acceptance criteria and revision rounds (2 standard), compensation with milestone payments (never 100% back-loaded), timeline with buffer for review cycles. Critical IP section: work-for-hire declaration AND backup IP assignment clause (work-for-hire alone has gaps for independent contractors under copyright law -- only 9 enumerated categories qualify), pre-existing IP carve-out for designer's tools and templates, moral rights waiver where permitted. Should cover independent contractor classification (behavioral control, financial control, relationship type per IRS factors), kill fee provision (15-25% of remaining value if client terminates for convenience), and deliverable acceptance (5-10 business day review, deemed accepted if no written rejection). Should flag 'The Infinite Revision Loop' anti-pattern.

input: A potential client sent us their contract to sign. How should I review it for unfavorable terms? What red flags should I watch for?
ideal: Response should provide a systematic review framework: first check for the 10 red flags (no liability cap, one-sided indemnification, vague deliverables, no termination for convenience, IP assignment of pre-existing work, auto-renewal with short cancellation window, unfavorable jurisdiction, no dispute escalation, payment terms beyond Net-60, no force majeure). Should recommend categorizing every clause using the Must-Have/Should-Have/Nice-to-Have/Concedable framework before responding. Should warn about 'The Template Blindness' anti-pattern -- their template was drafted to favor them. Specific advice: never sign without redlining, create a comparison document showing their terms vs market standard, identify their likely Must-Haves from the redline pattern, and prepare a trade matrix. Should emphasize attorney review for contracts over $10K or with unusual risk profiles.

input: We're launching a SaaS product and need Terms of Service. What sections are required and what legal protections should we include?
ideal: Response should cover the 10 core ToS sections: acceptance of terms (clickwrap preferred over browsewrap for enforceability, age restrictions, organizational authority), account terms (registration, security, termination), acceptable use policy (prohibited activities, resource limits, enforcement), user content (license grant from users, content ownership retained by users, moderation rights, DMCA procedures), intellectual property (provider IP rights, trademark usage, feedback license), payment and billing (subscription terms, auto-renewal with clear disclosure, refund policy, 30-day price change notice), service availability (SLA reference, maintenance windows, force majeure), limitation of liability (adapted for consumer context), dispute resolution (mandatory arbitration if applicable, class action waiver, governing law), modification of terms (30-day minimum notice, continued use as acceptance). Must cover DMCA safe harbor requirements: designate agent with Copyright Office, publish takedown/counter-notice procedures, implement repeat infringer policy. Should emphasize privacy policy alignment with ToS.

input: Our client wants to expand the scope of an ongoing project. How should we handle this change order?
ideal: Response should cover the change order process: reference parent MSA and SOW being amended, detailed description of what is being added/removed/modified with measurable specifics, impact on scope (new deliverables with acceptance criteria), impact on timeline (revised milestones, new delivery date), impact on fees (additional costs, modified payment schedule, new total contract value), new assumptions created by the change, and authorization (both parties sign before work begins). Should emphasize the critical rule: no work begins on changed scope until the change order is signed by both parties. Should reference the SOW out-of-scope section to distinguish change orders from items that were always excluded. Should warn about 'The Scope Creep Enabler' anti-pattern and the consequence of verbal change approvals (disputed invoices).

input: I need to write an IP assignment clause for a contractor building our mobile app. What should it cover?
ideal: Response should explain the work-for-hire doctrine limitation: under U.S. copyright law, work-for-hire applies to independent contractors only for 9 enumerated categories. Software development typically does not fall under these categories, so a work-for-hire clause alone is insufficient. Must include both a work-for-hire provision AND a backup IP assignment clause. The assignment should cover: all work product, inventions, and deliverables created during the engagement, waiver of moral rights where permitted, pre-existing IP carve-out (contractor's existing tools, libraries, frameworks remain theirs with perpetual license to client), assignment of all rights including copyright, patent, trade secret, obligation to execute future documents to perfect assignment, and representation that work is original and does not infringe third-party IP. Should flag the red flag of assignment clauses that capture pre-existing work.

input: I'm reviewing a contract and the termination clause only allows termination for cause with a 60-day cure period. Is this a problem?
ideal: Response should identify this as a significant red flag: no termination for convenience means you can only exit by proving material breach, which requires documentation, the 60-day cure period (double the standard 30 days), and potentially litigation. This is 'The Perpetual Lock-In' anti-pattern. Should recommend negotiating: add termination for convenience with 30-60 day written notice for both parties, reduce cure period to 30 days (industry standard), include wind-down obligations (payment for work completed, return of materials, transition assistance), add kill fee provision if applicable, and include immediate termination rights for insolvency or bankruptcy. Should explain that without convenience termination, the only exit is waiting for contract expiration or manufacturing a breach claim -- both are costly. Should note that asymmetric termination rights (they can exit, you cannot) are a walkaway criterion.

input: We're subcontracting part of a client project to a specialized vendor. What contract structure do we need?
ideal: Response should address the multi-layered contract structure: your MSA/SOW with the client (upstream agreement) and your agreement with the subcontractor (downstream agreement). The subcontractor agreement must include: flow-down of relevant client obligations (confidentiality, IP assignment, delivery standards), back-to-back payment terms (you are not the bank -- subcontractor payment triggered by client payment to you, within reasonable bounds), IP assignment from subcontractor to you (so you can assign to client per upstream agreement), non-solicitation (subcontractor cannot go directly to your client), confidentiality covering both your information and client information, deliverable acceptance criteria mirroring client SOW requirements, and your right to direct work product modifications. Should warn about ensuring your upstream agreement permits subcontracting (some MSAs prohibit it or require written consent). Should cover liability flow: your liability to client does not reduce because you subcontracted.

input: A project got cancelled halfway through. The client says they only owe for completed work. We don't have a kill fee clause. What can we do?
ideal: Response should address the immediate situation and the systemic fix. Immediate: review the contract termination clause -- if it allows termination for convenience, check for wind-down payment obligations. If it specifies payment for work completed, the client's position may be contractually correct. Check if the contract addresses work-in-progress (partially completed deliverables). Review whether the payment schedule has milestone payments already earned but not yet invoiced. For work completed, invoice immediately with detailed documentation. For the systemic fix: every future contract should include a kill fee provision (15-25% of remaining contract value upon client convenience termination), payment for all work completed and in-progress at the time of termination, reimbursement of non-cancellable expenses committed to the project, and a reasonable wind-down period. This is the consequence of 'The Handshake Deal' pattern -- even written contracts without kill fees leave the service provider absorbing 100% of cancellation risk.

input: A client is pushing back hard on our liability cap and wants unlimited liability from us. How do I negotiate this?
ideal: Response should provide a structured negotiation approach. First, understand their interest: they want financial protection if something goes wrong. Acknowledge this is reasonable. Then explain why unlimited liability is a walkaway criterion: a $50K project should not create $5M exposure -- the risk-reward is inverted. Offer graduated alternatives: (1) standard 12-month fee cap with carve-outs for IP infringement and confidentiality breaches at a higher multiplier (2x or 3x fees), (2) require professional liability insurance at a specific coverage level ($1M-$2M) named as additional insured, (3) propose a performance bond or escrow for high-value deliverables, (4) offer a higher liability cap (24-month fees) in exchange for other favorable terms. Use the trade matrix: 'We can increase the liability cap to 24 months of fees if you agree to mutual indemnification and Net-30 payment terms.' If client insists on unlimited liability after alternatives: this is a walkaway per the quantified criteria (liability exposure exceeds 3x contract value). State your position clearly and offer to revisit.

input: We need to negotiate a contract renewal with better terms. Our current contract auto-renews in 90 days. What's our strategy?
ideal: Response should provide a renewal negotiation strategy. Pre-negotiation: review current contract for all terms you want changed, benchmark current pricing against market rates, document value delivered during the current term (quantified outcomes), identify your BATNA (alternative providers or bringing capability in-house), check the auto-renewal cancellation window (must act before it closes or you are locked in for another term). Strategy: send written notice within the cancellation window stating intent to renegotiate (this preserves your right to exit if negotiations fail while keeping the relationship open). Prepare a priority list using the clause framework: Must-Haves for the renewal (updated pricing, improved payment terms, added convenience termination), Should-Haves (updated SLA tiers, expanded scope), Nice-to-Haves (reporting improvements, review cadence). Present renewal as a partnership evolution, not a threat. Should warn about 'The Perpetual Lock-In' anti-pattern in the current contract and recommend negotiating shorter auto-renewal periods and longer cancellation windows for the new term.
